1	//! `SubcommandRunner` trait
2	//!
3	//! The purpose is to simplify invoking a subcommand so `main`
4	//! can invoke any subcommand with one line:
5	//!
6	//! `Commands::Chid(args) => args.dispatch(&ctx),`
7	//!
8	//! Instead of the ~11 lines that the default `dispatch` below
9	//! now encapsulates.
10	//!
11	//! Implementors provide:
12	//!
13	//! - [`SubcommandRunner::to_params`] — build the clap-free
14	//!   `Params` from the clap `Args` (covers both `From` /
15	//!   `TryFrom` shapes uniformly via `Result<_, String>`).
16	//! - [`SubcommandRunner::run`] — the subcommand body.
17	//!
18	//! The default [`SubcommandRunner::dispatch`] builds `Params`
19	//! via `to_params`, runs via `run`, and maps the result to
20	//! `ExitCode`. Errors at any stage log via `error!` and return
21	//! `ExitCode::FAILURE`. `Context` is loaded once in `main` and
22	//! passed in by reference so a single `Context` is shared across
23	//! the (one) match arm that actually runs.
24	//!
25	//! ## See also
26	//!
27	//! - The wider CLI-args / subcommand-layer split this trait
28	//!   formalizes: [`ARCHITECTURE.md > args → Context + Params`][arch].
29	//! - Cycle design, ladder, evaluation gate:
30	//!   [`chores-10.md > 0.50.0-0`][open].
31	//! - Worked example, trait-shape decisions, naming:
32	//!   [`chores-10.md > 0.50.0-1`][port].
33	//! - First implementor: [`crate::chid`].
34	//!
35	//! [arch]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md#args--context--params
36	//! [open]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/blob/main/notes/chores/chores-10.md#chore-open-subcommand-trait-sweep-0500-0
37	//! [port]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/blob/main/notes/chores/chores-10.md#refactor-subcommandrunner-trait--chid-0500-1
38	
39	use std::process::ExitCode;
40	
41	use log::error;
42	
43	use crate::context::Context;
44	
45	/// Trait implemented by every subcommand's clap `Args` type so
46	/// `main.rs` can dispatch via a single `args.dispatch(&ctx)`
47	/// call.
48	pub trait SubcommandRunner {
49	    /// The clap-free `Params` struct the subcommand body
50	    /// consumes.
51	    type Params;
52	
53	    /// Build `Params` from `Args`. `Result<_, String>` absorbs
54	    /// both `From` (return `Ok(...)`) and `TryFrom` (forward
55	    /// the error) shapes uniformly.
56	    fn to_params(&self) -> Result<Self::Params, String>;
57	
58	    /// Run the subcommand body.
59	    fn run(ctx: &Context, params: &Self::Params) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>;
60	
61	    /// Default dispatch: build `Params` via `to_params`, bracket
62	    /// the run with `crate::bm_track` enter/exit, execute via
63	    /// `run`, and map the result to `ExitCode`. Errors at any
64	    /// stage log via `error!` and return `ExitCode::FAILURE`.
65	    /// `bm_track` itself emits at `debug!`, so default runs stay
66	    /// quiet and the detached `finalize --exec` child (which runs
67	    /// without `-v`) silently no-ops — no per-call gate is needed
68	    /// here. `surface_previous_failures` for finalize's failure
69	    /// markers is handled in `main` before dispatch, so dispatch
70	    /// has no session-chrome responsibility at all anymore.
71	    fn dispatch(&self, ctx: &Context) -> ExitCode {
72	        let params = match self.to_params() {
73	            Ok(p) => p,
74	            Err(e) => {
75	                error!("{e}");
76	                return ExitCode::FAILURE;
77	            }
78	        };
79	
80	        // Command name is the first positional after the binary;
81	        // clap has already validated it by the time we reach
82	        // dispatch (top-level parse errors exit earlier).
83	        let command_name = std::env::args().nth(1).unwrap_or_else(|| "?".to_string()); // OK: default when somehow invoked without a subcommand
84	
85	        crate::bm_track("enter", &command_name);
86	        let exit_code = match Self::run(ctx, &params) {
87	            Ok(()) => ExitCode::SUCCESS,
88	            Err(e) => {
89	                error!("{e}");
90	                ExitCode::FAILURE
91	            }
92	        };
93	        crate::bm_track("exit ", &command_name);
94	        exit_code
95	    }
96	}
97

590	        )
591	        .into());
592	    }
593	    Ok(())
594	}
595	
596	/// Entry point for the `push` subcommand.
597	///
598	/// 0.37.0-5 behavior: interactive flow with two approval gates
599	/// (review + message) plus polish flags: `--dry-run` prints what
600	/// would run without side effects, `--step` pauses between every
601	/// stage, non-tty stdin fails fast when prompts are required,
602	/// `--yes` opts out of all prompts. On any failure in stages 4-6
603	/// (the local mutation window between `commit-app` and
604	/// `bookmark-set`), both repos roll back to the `jj op` snapshot
605	/// recorded at the start of `commit-app`. After `push-app` the
606	/// remote boundary is crossed and recovery is forward-only.
607	///
608	/// `ctx` is unused today (push reads `.vc-config.toml` for its
609	/// state-file layout and hard-codes the finalize `--log` path); it's
610	/// present for the uniform subcommand-layer signature.
611	pub fn push(_ctx: &Context, params: &PushParams) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
612	    let cwd = std::env::current_dir()?;
613	    push_in(&cwd, params)
614	}
615	
616	/// `push` parameterized on the workspace root. CLI dispatch calls
617	/// this with `std::env::current_dir()`; integration tests call it
618	/// with a fixture tempdir so the stage bodies mutate the fixture's
619	/// repos instead of the developer's working tree.
620	pub(crate) fn push_in(

input.description: Smoke-check squash-push help output

content.content:
vc-x1-dev 0.69.0-2

Squash `@` into `@-`, advance a bookmark, and push.

Captures a repo's trailing working-copy writes into the last
commit and publishes it — rewriting an already-pushed commit,
so the push is a forced update. Built for the bot's `.claude`
session repo (the session tail); also useful on the app repo
as a deliberate amend-and-push.

Zero-ceremony default: bare `vc-x1 squash-push` squashes
@ → @- and pushes `main` in `.`. With an empty `@` the squash
is skipped; if the bookmark already matches the remote the
command reports "already sync'd" and exits 0.

Usage: vc-x1-dev squash-push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance and push
          
          [default: main]

Options:
  -R, --repo <REPO>
          Path to jj repo
          
          [default: .]

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --squash [<SOURCE,TARGET>]
          Squash SOURCE into TARGET [default: @,@-]

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

input.old_string:
- 0.69.0-2 squash-push: rename `finalize` → `squash-push` (current)
  - zero-ceremony default: `-R .`, BOOKMARK positional
    defaulting to `main`, no-op feedback
  - retire `--detach` / `--delay`
  - module + types follow: `finalize.rs` →
    `squash_push.rs`, `FinalizeArgs` / `FinalizeParams` →
    `SquashPushArgs` / `SquashPushParams`
  - `finalize_inline` dissolves: with the detach branch
    gone, preflight + exec is the command's only path, so
    the shim merges into the renamed entry point
  - decide the detach failure-marker machinery
    (`write_failure_marker` / `surface_previous_failures`):
    retire with `--detach`, but consider keeping the
    surfacing one cycle to drain markers from older
    installed versions
  - rename push stage `finalize-claude` →
    `squash-push-claude`; `--no-finalize` →
    `--no-squash-push`
  - decide deprecated `finalize` alias

content.text:
Work complete. Please review.

**Summary of 0.69.0-2 — rename `finalize` → `squash-push`:**

- `src/finalize.rs` → `src/squash_push.rs`; `FinalizeArgs`/`FinalizeParams` → `SquashPushArgs`/`SquashPushParams`. The command is now `vc-x1 squash-push [BOOKMARK] [-R <repo>] [--squash SOURCE,TARGET]` — BOOKMARK defaults to `main`, `-R` to `.`, `--squash` stays as an optional pair override defaulting to `@,@-`.
- `--detach`, `--delay`, `--exec`, and `--push` are retired, along with `detach()`, `build_exec_args()`, and the delay sleep. `finalize_inline` dissolved: `squash_push()` (preflight + execute) is the single entry point, used by both the CLI and push's stage.
- No-op feedback per the design notes: empty undescribed `@` + bookmark at both target and remote → "already sync'd", exit 0; empty `@` with remote behind → skips the squash, still pushes.
- Push stage renamed `finalize-claude` → `squash-push-claude`; `--no-finalize` → `--no-squash-push`; `Stage::FinalizeClaude` → `Stage::SquashPushClaude`.

**The two "decide" items** (also recorded in the plan ladder):

- **Failure markers**: `write_failure_marker` retired (nothing detaches anymore); `surface_previous_failures` kept one cycle to drain markers older installed versions may still write, called unconditionally in `main` now.
- **`finalize` alias**: none. The flag surface changed incompatibly (`--push`/`--detach`/`--delay` gone), so an alias would turn a clear "unrecognized subcommand" into confusing per-flag errors. A test pins the retirement.

**One deviation to flag**: `Context.log`'s only reader was the detach re-exec, so clippy's dead-code lint forced a call — I removed the field and the `load(log)` parameter (`src/context.rs`, with doc note) rather than `#[allow]`-ing it. Also, `tests/cli_sync.rs` (outside `src/`) invoked `push --no-finalize` and needed the rename.

Cargo cycle: fmt, clippy `-D warnings`, and all 413 tests clean; `vc-x1-dev` 0.69.0-2 installed and `squash-push --help` renders the intended zero-ceremony surface. One stale-state caveat worth knowing: an old `.vc-x1/push-state.toml` recording `finalize-claude` will fail stage parsing and need `--restart` — I did not add a legacy name mapping.

A symlink `CLAUDE.md → AGENTS.md` also satisfies Claude
Code's loader, but the `@AGENTS.md` import was chosen
because it is explicit and visible in a diff and does not
depend on symlink semantics (git symlink handling,
cross-platform checkout). Separately, GitHub renders a
symlinked `.md` as a stub page rather than the target's
markdown, so a `CLAUDE.md#anchor` link through a symlink
would not resolve — moot here since live links now point
straight at `AGENTS.md`, but it reinforces keeping
`CLAUDE.md` a real file.

## docs: tighten after-finalize rule (0.63.1)

Commits: [[2]]

The stop-and-wait rule in `cycle-protocol.md` was titled
`### After finalize: stop and wait` and phrased as "final
words go in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize"
— wording that assumes finalize is a separate,
manually-invoked step and names only finalize as the
trigger. With `vc-x1 push` the push and `vc-x1 finalize` on
`.claude` are bundled as the tail stages, so there is no
"before finalize" gap left to speak in once the push is
invoked. After `0.63.0`'s push the bot emitted a closing
summary *after* `vc-x1 push` returned, violating the rule
(and likely riding into the `.claude` commit via the
`--delay 10` window).

- **Renamed to "After push or finalize"** — both triggers
  are named, in the title and the paragraph: the remote
  crossing is the push itself, and `vc-x1 finalize` on
  `.claude` is the detached step. This also covers a manual
  `jj git push` (push, no finalize) and a manual
  `vc-x1 finalize` (no wrapper).
- **Wrapper case spelled out** — `vc-x1 push` does both as
  tail stages (`push-app`, then `vc-x1 finalize` on

input.old_string:
### After push or finalize: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
stop for the whole turn.

`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit anything
after the tool returns.

Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
squashes → pushes):

- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
  the invoke — into the snapshot.
- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
  the bot to do more work.
- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
  after the tool returns.

**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
saying, say it *before* the invoke.
input.new_string:
### After push or squash-push: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) — via the
`vc-x1 push` wrapper or by hand — or run **squash-push** on
the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`), you
**MUST NEVER** proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text
output) until the user explicitly directs you to continue.
**Even when the next step seems obvious — wait.** Treat
push-or-squash-push as a hard stop for the whole turn.

`vc-x1 push` publishes the session repo itself — its tail
stage `squash-push-claude` squashes `.claude`'s trailing
session writes and pushes `main` in-process — so there is no
gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit
anything after the tool returns.

Why (`.claude` is a live journal the squash folds → pushes):

- The push captures everything written up to the invocation,
  but the invocation's own record — and anything emitted
  after it — lands in `@` *after* the squash. Everything the
  bot does post-push grows that tail.
- The empty-`@` goal is self-referential for the bot: its own
  squash-push is itself session data, so its `@` is non-empty
  again immediately. The bot forgoes empty-`@`; only the
  user, acting after the turn, can capture the full tail
  (run `vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`).

**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after the push
returned. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
saying, say it *before* the invoke.

input.old_string:
### Recovery

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
  finalize by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
  ```

- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
  extra activity by the bot's agents).
  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
    *after* the snapshot.
  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
    change; it is not under the user's control.)
  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
    - Verified 0.64.0: two finalizes were executed and
      we purposely used the bot again and the second finalize
      captured everything.
  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave

  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
  stale stage.

### Recovery

- **If push exits before `squash-push-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `squash-push-claude`), run
  it by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude
  ```

  It runs in-process, so a failure is a visible non-zero
  exit — no log file to chase.
- **Run squash-push again if `@` is non-empty** after a
  pass (also desirable after extra activity by the bot's
  agents).
  - Why: the bot keeps writing session data while the
    command runs — the invocation's own record plus any
    closing response land after the squash.
  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
    change; it is not under the user's control.)
  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
    and land after the pass. The remedy is the same: just
    run squash-push again. This is why a single pass is never
    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
  stale stage. A pre-0.69.0-2 state file names the retired
  `finalize-claude` stage and also needs `--restart`.

content.text:
Docs updated. Summary of this round:

**cycle-protocol.md** — the two -4-planned sections rewritten for the inline world:

- **"After push or squash-push: stop and wait"** — the rule survives, the rationale changed. Triggers are now the push itself and a manual `vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`. The `--detach`/`--delay 10` bullets are gone; the new "why" is the self-referential tail from the In Progress problem statement: the bot's own squash-push is session data, so its `@` is non-empty again immediately — only you, acting after the turn, can capture the full tail. The "Known slip" warning stays.
- **"Recovery"** — the hand-run recipe is now just `vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude` (in-process, visible failure, no log file). The "run again if `@` non-empty" bullet keeps the append-only/safe-to-repeat and no-guarantees points; I dropped the "Verified 0.64.0: two finalizes captured everything" sub-bullet since it documented the detached mechanism (it survives in git/chores history — flag if you want it kept). The stale-state bullet gained the pre-0.69.0-2 `finalize-claude` state-file caveat.
- Also the `.claude` cadence line: "the finalize commit" → "the squash-push fold".

**AGENTS.md** — the only two "finalize" occurrences were plain-English uses about `jj commit`; reworded to "seal" so the word no longer collides with the retired command name.

**todo.md** — -2's ladder gained a "docs pulled forward from -4" bullet; -4 slimmed to README only; the Continuity note about unsandboxed test runs updated to record that you've since allowed `~/.config/jj`, `/tmp/`, and `~/.cargo` in the sandbox.

Ready for review — code and docs together are the full -2 diff (`jj diff` to see it all).

input.old_string:
### After push or squash-push: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) — via the
`vc-x1 push` wrapper or by hand — or run **squash-push** on
the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`), you
**MUST NEVER** proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text
output) until the user explicitly directs you to continue.
**Even when the next step seems obvious — wait.** Treat
push-or-squash-push as a hard stop for the whole turn.

`vc-x1 push` publishes the session repo itself — its tail
stage `squash-push-claude` squashes `.claude`'s trailing
session writes and pushes `main` in-process — so there is no
gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit
anything after the tool returns.

Why (`.claude` is a live journal the squash folds → pushes):

- The push captures everything written up to the invocation,
  but the invocation's own record — and anything emitted
  after it — lands in `@` *after* the squash. Everything the
  bot does post-push grows that tail.
- The empty-`@` goal is self-referential for the bot: its own
  squash-push is itself session data, so its `@` is non-empty
  again immediately. The bot forgoes empty-`@`; only the
  user, acting after the turn, can capture the full tail
  (run `vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`).

**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after the push
returned. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
saying, say it *before* the invoke.
input.new_string:
### After push or squash-push: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) — via the
`vc-x1 push` wrapper or by hand — or run **squash-push** on
the bot repo (`vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`), the default
is a hard stop for the whole turn: do not proceed (next
step, edit, tool call, text output) until the user directs
you to continue. **Even when the next step seems obvious —
wait.**

The stop is scoped to the user's directive, not to the push
itself: when a standing directive already covers more work —
"finish the remaining ladder commits on your own", a
multi-step task delegated up front — an intermediate push is
just a step, and the bot proceeds with the directed work.
The hard stop lands on the turn's *final* push, when the
directive is fulfilled and nothing directed remains.

`vc-x1 push` publishes the bot repo itself — its tail stage
`squash-push-claude` squashes the bot repo's trailing
session writes and pushes `main` in-process — so on the
final push there is no gap left to speak in once it is
invoked. Put **all** closing words *before* that invoke; do
not purposely emit anything after the tool returns.

Why (the bot repo is a live journal the squash folds →
pushes):

- The push captures everything written up to the invocation;
  everything after it — the invocation's own record, any
  closing words — lands in `@` as a trailing tail.
- Between the pushes of a delegated multi-step task that
  tail is harmless: it rides into the next cycle's bot
  commit on the normal cadence.
- At the turn's final push the tail has no next commit to
  ride into, and the empty-`@` goal is self-referential for
  the bot: its own squash-push is itself session data, so
  its `@` is non-empty again immediately. The bot forgoes
  empty-`@`; only the user, acting after the turn, can
  capture the full tail (run `vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`).

**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after the
turn's final push returned. There is no "harmless" closing
line — if it's worth saying, say it *before* the invoke.

content.text:
All three points addressed:

1. **Rule strength** — codified as: the hard stop is scoped to your directive, not the push. A standing directive covering more work ("finish the remaining ladder commits on your own") makes an intermediate push just a step; the stop lands on the turn's *final* push, when the directive is fulfilled. The "why" bullets now explain both cases: between-push tails ride into the next cycle's bot commit on the normal cadence; only the final push's tail has no next commit, which is what you capture post-turn.

2. **Terminology** — the rewritten sections now use "bot repo" (and "Late work-repo tweak" in Recovery). The full sweep — "bot repo"/"work repo" replacing "session repo"/"code repo"/"app repo" across AGENTS.md, cycle-protocol.md, and code doc comments — is recorded in -4's docs scope with the decision date.

3. **`squash-push-claude`** — it does exist in this commit: `Stage::SquashPushClaude` renders as `squash-push-claude` in `push.rs`, and `vc-x1-dev push --help` shows it in the stage list and on `--no-squash-push`. The stable `vc-x1` on PATH is still 0.69.0-0 and shows `finalize-claude`, which is likely what you saw. The doc line stands unless you meant something else.

Ready for another review pass.

content.text: Condensing the whole section into the house intro-plus-bullets shape — the Why bullets fold into one, and the wrapper/silence paragraphs collapse:

input.old_string:
### After push or squash-push: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) — via the
`vc-x1 push` wrapper or by hand — or run **squash-push** on
the bot repo (`vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`), the default
is a hard stop for the whole turn: do not proceed (next
step, edit, tool call, text output) until the user directs
you to continue. **Even when the next step seems obvious —
wait.**

The stop is scoped to the user's directive, not to the push
itself: when a standing directive already covers more work —
"finish the remaining ladder commits on your own", a
multi-step task delegated up front — an intermediate push is
just a step, and the bot proceeds with the directed work.
The hard stop lands on the turn's *final* push, when the
directive is fulfilled and nothing directed remains.

`vc-x1 push` publishes the bot repo itself — its last stage
squashes the bot repo's trailing session writes and pushes
`main` in-process — so on the final push there is no gap
left to speak in once it is invoked. Put **all** closing
words *before* that invoke; do not purposely emit anything
after the tool returns.

Why (the bot repo is a live journal the squash folds →
pushes):

- The push captures everything written up to the invocation;
  everything after it — the invocation's own record, any
  closing words — lands in `@` as a trailing tail.
- Between the pushes of a delegated multi-step task that
  tail is harmless: it rides into the next cycle's bot
  commit on the normal cadence.
- At the turn's final push the tail has no next commit to
  ride into, and the empty-`@` goal is self-referential for
  the bot: its own squash-push is itself session data, so
  its `@` is non-empty again immediately. The bot forgoes
  empty-`@`; only the user, acting after the turn, can
  capture the full tail (run `vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`).

**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after the
turn's final push returned. There is no "harmless" closing
line — if it's worth saying, say it *before* the invoke.
input.new_string:
### After push or squash-push: stop and wait

After a **push** (crossing the remote boundary, by hand or
via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — whose last stage publishes
the bot repo too) or a manual **squash-push** on the bot
repo, stop for the turn: no next step, edit, tool call, or
text output until the user directs otherwise. **Even when
the next step seems obvious — wait.**

- **Scope**: the stop follows the user's directive, not the
  push. A standing directive covering more work ("finish
  the remaining ladder commits on your own") makes an
  intermediate push just a step; the hard stop lands on the
  turn's *final* push.
- **Why**: the bot repo is a live journal — everything after
  the invocation (its own record, closing words) lands in
  `@` as a trailing tail. Between delegated pushes the tail
  rides into the next cycle's bot commit; the final push's
  tail has no next commit, and the bot's own squash-push is
  itself session data (`@` refills immediately), so only the
  user, after the turn, can capture it
  (`vc-x1 squash-push -R .claude`).
- **Silence**: put all closing words *before* the final
  push. There is no "harmless" closing line after it — a
  known slip.

/// Squash `@` into `@-`, advance a bookmark, and push
    #[command(long_about = "Squash `@` into `@-`, advance a bookmark, and push.\n\n\
        Captures a repo's trailing working-copy writes into the last\n\
        commit and publishes it — rewriting an already-pushed commit,\n\
        so the push is a forced update. Built for the bot's `.claude`\n\
        session repo (the session tail); also useful on the app repo\n\
        as a deliberate amend-and-push.\n\n\
        Zero-ceremony default: bare `vc-x1 squash-push` squashes\n\
        @ → @- and pushes `main` in `.`. With an empty `@` the squash\n\
        is skipped; if the bookmark already matches the remote the\n\
        command reports \"already sync'd\" and exits 0.")]
    SquashPush(squash_push::SquashPushArgs),

    /// Dual-repo commit+push+squash-push in one resumable command
    #[command(
        long_about = "Dual-repo commit+push+squash-push in one resumable command.\n\n\
        Collapses the manual commit-push-publish ceremony into a\n\
        single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a\n\
        state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can\n\
        resume without re-doing completed stages.\n\n\
        Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)\n\
        → message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →\n\
        commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-set\n\
        (app → <bookmark>, session → main) → push-app →\n\
        squash-push-claude. Failures in commit-app / commit-claude /\n\
        bookmark-set roll both repos back via\n\
        `jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.\n\
        After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and\n\
        recovery is forward-only.\n\n\
        Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes\n\
        to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body\n\
        across resumes so only the first invocation needs them."
    )]
    Push(push::PushArgs),
}

41	/// Banner string emitted as the first line of normal command runs
42	/// and shown at the top of subcommand `--help` output. Built from
43	/// `Cargo.toml`'s name + version at compile time so it stays in
44	/// sync with the bumped version.
45	const BANNER: &str = concat!(env!("CARGO_PKG_NAME"), " ", env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"));
46	
47	/// Top-level about line — name, version, and the project tagline
48	/// on a single line. Used as the top-level `about` so `vc-x1 -h`
49	/// reads as one banner-plus-tagline header instead of two stacked
50	/// lines.
51	const TOP_ABOUT: &str = concat!(
52	    env!("CARGO_PKG_NAME"),
53	    " ",
54	    env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"),
55	    " - jj workspace tooling"
56	);
57	
58	/// Build the clap command tree with `BANNER` set as `before_help`
59	/// on every subcommand (transitively). Top-level skips `before_help`
60	/// because its own `about` already carries the name+version+tagline.
61	/// Walks via `mut_subcommand` so individual subcommand
62	/// `#[command(long_about = ...)]` blocks don't have to repeat the
63	/// banner text.
64	fn cli_with_banner() -> clap::Command {
65	    fn add_to_subs(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
66	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
67	            .get_subcommands()
68	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
69	            .collect();
70	        for name in names {
71	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
72	        }
73	        cmd
74	    }
75	    fn add_with_banner(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
76	        cmd = cmd.before_help(BANNER);
77	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
78	            .get_subcommands()
79	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
80	            .collect();
81	        for name in names {
82	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
83	        }
84	        cmd
85	    }
86	    add_to_subs(Cli::command())
87	}
88	
89	#[derive(Parser, Debug)]
90	#[command(about = TOP_ABOUT, max_term_width = 80)]
91	pub struct Cli {
92	    /// Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then
93	    /// continue. With no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
94	    ///
95	    /// Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after
96	    /// printing): the banner now rides along with normal
97	    /// subcommand execution rather than gating it, so scripts
98	    /// can capture the version *and* the command's output in
99	    /// one invocation.
100	    #[arg(short = 'V', long = "version", global = true, action = clap::ArgAction::SetTrue)]
101	    pub version: bool,
102

Done. The title line is now precise about all three actions:

- `vc-x1 -h`: **push — "Commit both repos, push the work repo, squash-push the bot repo"**
- `push --help` long form opens with: "Commit both repos, push the work repo's BOOKMARK, and squash-push the bot repo's `main` — one resumable command."
- While there I also switched the new-this-commit help text to the decided terms: the stage list's bookmark-set parenthetical now reads "(work repo → \<bookmark\>, bot repo → main)", and squash-push's long help says "Built for the bot repo (`.claude`, the session tail); also useful on the work repo".

Cargo cycle re-ran green (clippy clean, 393 tests, reinstalled).

One visible leftover in that help output: the BOOKMARK positional's own doc still says "Bookmark to advance in the code repo" — that's pre-existing text belonging to the -4 terminology sweep, but I can pull it into this commit if you'd rather the `push -h` page be internally consistent now.

message.content:
In squash-push -h "title" it's a little to specific it say '@' into `@-`:
Squash `@` into `@-`, advance a bookmark, and push

Maybe something like:"Squash <SOURCE> into <TARGET>, advance a bookmark and push. defaults SOURCE=@ TARGET=@-"

In squash-push -h "title" it's a little to specific it say '@' into `@-`:
Squash `@` into `@-`, advance a bookmark, and push

Maybe something like:
Squash <SOURCE> into <TARGET>, advance a bookmark and push. defaults SOURCE=@ TARGET=@-

Although maybe a little long, thoughts

content.text:
Agreed on the direction — the defaults clause makes the one-liner long, and it's already documented twice on the same page (`--squash`'s `[default: @,@-]` and the "Zero-ceremony default" paragraph). So I'd keep the short line generic and let the long form carry the defaults:

- short: `Squash SOURCE into TARGET, advance a bookmark, and push`
- long opener: adds "(defaults: SOURCE=@, TARGET=@-)"

Applying that:

65	    fn add_to_subs(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
66	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
67	            .get_subcommands()
68	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
69	            .collect();
70	        for name in names {
71	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
72	        }
73	        cmd
74	    }
75	    fn add_with_banner(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
76	        cmd = cmd.before_help(BANNER);
77	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
78	            .get_subcommands()
79	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
80	            .collect();
81	        for name in names {
82	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
83	        }
84	        cmd
85	    }
86	    add_to_subs(Cli::command())
87	}
88	
89	#[derive(Parser, Debug)]
90	#[command(about = TOP_ABOUT, max_term_width = 80)]
91	pub struct Cli {
92	    /// Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then
93	    /// continue. With no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
94	    ///
95	    /// Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after
96	    /// printing): the banner now rides along with normal
97	    /// subcommand execution rather than gating it, so scripts
98	    /// can capture the version *and* the command's output in
99	    /// one invocation.
100	    #[arg(short = 'V', long = "version", global = true, action = clap::ArgAction::SetTrue)]
101	    pub version: bool,
102	
103	    /// Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace
104	    #[arg(short, long, global = true, action = clap::ArgAction::Count)]
105	    pub verbose: u8,
106	
107	    /// Log file path (captures all levels)
108	    #[arg(long, global = true)]
109	    pub log: Option<std::path::PathBuf>,
110	
111	    #[command(subcommand)]
112	    pub(crate) command: Option<Commands>,
113	}

133	        Status labels:\n  \
134	          ok   — ochid trailer is valid\n  \
135	          err  — ochid has issues (wrong prefix, wrong length, ID not found)\n  \
136	          miss — no ochid trailer; shows match from other repo if found"
137	    )]
138	    ValidateDesc(validate_desc::ValidateDescArgs),
139	
140	    /// Fix commit descriptions against the other repo (dry-run by default)
141	    #[command(long_about = "Fix commit descriptions against the other repo.\n\n\
142	        Default is dry-run; use --no-dry-run to write changes.\n\n\
143	        Output columns: STATUS  CHANGEID  TITLE  [DETAILS]\n\n\
144	        Status labels:\n  \
145	          ok    — ochid trailer is valid (no change)\n  \
146	          fix   — ochid has issues, shows proposed fix (dry-run)\n  \
147	          fixed — ochid was rewritten (--no-dry-run)\n  \
148	          add   — missing ochid, match found, shows proposed addition (dry-run)\n  \
149	          added — missing ochid was added (--no-dry-run)\n  \
150	          skip  — skipped (no ochid, no match, or max-fixes reached)\n  \
151	          err   — ID not found and no --fallback provided")]
152	    FixDesc(fix_desc::FixDescArgs),
153	
154	    /// Check todo-file entry numbering and indent
155	    #[command(
156	        long_about = "Check a todo file's `## Todo` and `## Bugs` entry numbering.\n\n\
157	        Verifies each section is numbered 1..N in document order and\n\
158	        that continuation-line indent matches the number-prefix width.\n\
159	        Read-only; exits non-zero if any entry needs fixing — use\n\
160	        `fix-todo` to rewrite."
161	    )]
162	    ValidateTodo(validate_todo::ValidateTodoArgs),
163	
164	    /// Renumber todo-file entries (dry-run by default)

167	        Renumbers each section 1..N in document order and normalizes\n\

170	        --no-dry-run to write the file in place."
171	    )]
172	    FixTodo(fix_todo::FixTodoArgs),
173

185	        Repo set is resolved (in order):\n  \

193	          - diverged          rebase local onto remote; fail on conflicts\n  \

content.text:
Clippy clean, all test suites green, reinstalled. `surface_previous_failures`, `status_dir`, and the `main.rs` call are gone; removing the call also let `main` return the dispatch match directly (clippy flagged the now-pointless `let result = …; result`). The module doc and the ladder's decision bullet now say the marker machinery retired entirely with `--detach`, and that stale markers under `~/.cache/vc-x1/finalize-status` are inert — you can `rm -r` that directory whenever.

One follow-on for your call: `notes/bugs.md` #3 — "`finalize::surface_previous_failures` is racy" — is now moot since the function no longer exists. Retiring it would shift bugs #4/#5 down to #3/#4; the code comments only cite Bugs #1 and #2, so nothing dangles. Want me to prune it as part of this commit, or leave it for close-out?

message.content: Yes, prune it now

content.content:
1	# Bugs
2	
3	Known defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for.
4	Each entry describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of
5	the failure. Entries are numbered (`1.` `2.` …) the same way
6	as `## Todo` in `todo.md`; run
7	`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/bugs.md` to renumber after
8	insert / delete / reorder.
9	
10	## Bugs
11	
12	1. **`vc-x1 push`: the session-repo push silently never happens
13	   when the bot runs push sandboxed.** Observed in
14	   [tprobe](https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe) 2026-07-14:
15	   both local repos consistent and the GitHub code repo current,
16	   but [tprobe.claude](https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe.claude)
17	   stuck 8 commits behind (`jj bookmark list --all-remotes -R
18	   .claude`: `main@origin (behind by 8 commits)`).
19	   - **Root cause (diagnosed 2026-07-14).** Push doesn't push
20	     `.claude` itself; its `finalize-claude` stage spawns a
21	     detached child that sleeps 10s first:
22	     - the child — `vc-x1 finalize --squash --push main
23	       --delay 10 --detach` — is `setsid`'d to outlive push
24	     - a sandboxed Bash command kills every process in its pid
25	       namespace at command exit; `setsid` escapes the process
26	       group, not the namespace
27	     - the child dies mid-sleep, before its first `jj` command
28	     - reproduced in this project's sandbox with a bare `setsid`
29	       child; the bot thinks the same kill explains tprobe,
30	       whose sandbox is always-on (`autoAllowBashIfSandboxed:
31	       true`, `allowUnsandboxedCommands: false`) with
32	       `vc-x1 push *` permission-allowed
33	   - **Evidence.**
34	     - tprobe app repo: 8 `push bookmark main` ops — the
35	       in-process `push-app` stage worked; network was fine
36	     - tprobe `.claude`: zero squash/push ops since clone — the
37	       child never ran even one command
38	     - tprobe `.vc-x1/` state dir empty — every push "completed
39	       all stages"
40	   - **Why it's silent.**
41	     - SIGKILL — the child never writes a failure marker
42	     - marker writes to `~/.cache/vc-x1` are outside the sandbox
43	       write allowlist anyway; `write_failure_marker` swallows
44	       errors by design
45	     - `verify_completion_sanity` checks local bookmarks only
46	     - related: `CliLogger::new` panics (`.expect`) when the
47	       `--log` path can't be opened — a sandbox denying
48	       `/tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log` kills the child at startup
49	   - **Side effect:** the trailing-data squash never runs; the
50	     tail rides into the next cycle's session commit (misplaced,
51	     not lost).
52	   - **Fix direction:** see the Todo "push: inline the
53	     session-repo push; finalize becomes the user's empty-@
54	     tidy-up".
55	   - **Recovery** for an affected project: `jj git push
56	     --bookmark main -R <proj>/.claude` from an unsandboxed
57	     shell (or any in-process command that finishes before its
58	     sandbox exits).
59	
60	2. **`finalize` squash silently drops the source journal's
61	   ochids.** `finalize_exec` (`src/finalize.rs`) always runs
62	   `jj squash --ignore-immutable --use-destination-message
63	   --from <source> --into <target>`, so when the source
64	   commit's message carries `ochid:` trailers the squash
65	   discards them with the rest of the message — the
66	   destination's message wins unconditionally.
67	   - **When it bites:** the squash source (`@`) is a
68	     *described* journal instead of a bare trailing-data
69	     snapshot. Observed sequence (fc op log, 2026-06-08):
70	     the bot wrote the journal message via
71	     `jj describe wyrlsuusnyzz` while that change was still
72	     the uncommitted `@`; finalize then squashed
73	     `--from @ --into @-` where `@-` was the previous,
74	     already-pushed journal — destination message won,
75	     journal message + 6 ochids discarded. Finalize's
76	     assumption (`@` is disposable, `@-` holds the real
77	     message) inverts whenever the journal is described on
78	     `@` rather than committed first.
79	   - **Cost:** every code-side commit pointing at the
80	     squashed journal is left with a dangling
81	     `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` — the code↔session cross-link
82	     breaks. Recovery requires op-log surgery on the machine
83	     that still has the original object (this happened in the
84	     fc project: journal `wyrlsuusnyzz`, 6 dangling app
85	     ochids, recovered 2026-06-10 from a backup workspace's
86	     op log plus a `.claude main` force-push).
87	   - **Fix direction:** before squashing, detect `ochid:`
88	     trailers in the source message that the destination
89	     lacks; refuse (or merge the trailers into the
90	     destination message) instead of dropping them.
91	
92	3. **`finalize::surface_previous_failures` is racy and
93	   bounded by "next vc-x1 run".** The current model has
94	   four gaps:
95	   - **Stale forever.** Markers sit on disk until the
96	     next `vc-x1 <anything>` runs. If the user abandons
97	     the workspace (e.g., CI / scheduled use), failures
98	     are never surfaced.
99	   - **Concurrent surface_previous_failures.** Two
100	     `vc-x1` runs racing: both `read_dir`, both print
101	     the marker, one deletes (the other's `remove_file`
102	     silently fails). The user sees the same failure
103	     printed twice.
104	   - **Mid-write torn read.** A `finalize --exec` child
105	     writing a marker while a sibling `vc-x1` is
106	     surfacing could read partial content. Atomic-rename
107	     on write would close this.
108	   - **No notify-at-failure path.** A detached
109	     `finalize --exec` failure only becomes visible when
110	     the user next runs *any* `vc-x1` command — fine for
111	     interactive use, invisible for CI / scheduled use
112	     where there may be no next run.
113	
114	   The exec-child gate in `main.rs` (since 0.52.0-3)
115	   patches one related case — the detached child eating
116	   its own prior markers before the user can see them —
117	   but doesn't address any of the above. Holistic fix
118	   needs locking + atomic writes + maybe a
119	   notify-at-failure path for hands-off use.
120	
121	4. **`init --repo local` bare remotes keep HEAD at
122	   `refs/heads/master`.** The only branch pushed is `main`,
123	   so a later `jj git clone` of that bare repo has no default
124	   branch to auto-track and `vc-x1 clone` fails its
125	   `verify_tracking` check ("bookmark 'main' has non-tracking
126	   remote 'main@origin'"). Found building `tests/cli_sync.rs`
127	   (worked around there with `git symbolic-ref HEAD
128	   refs/heads/main`).
129	   - **Fix direction:** init's local bare provisioning sets
130	     HEAD to `refs/heads/main` at creation.
131	
132	5. **`clone` session-remote derivation mismatches init's
133	   local naming; relative TARGET breaks the session clone.**
134	   Two related defects, both found building
135	   `tests/cli_sync.rs`:
136	   - `derive_session_url` maps `<x>.git` →
137	     `<x>.claude.git`, but `init --repo local` names the
138	     session remote `remote-claude.git` — so a dual clone of
139	     a locally-init'd project's `remote-code.git` looks for
140	     `remote-code.claude.git` and fails.
141	   - A relative local-path TARGET fails on the session side
142	     regardless: `clone_dual` runs the session `jj git
143	     clone` with the just-cloned code repo as cwd, so the
144	     relative source no longer resolves. Workaround: pass an
145	     absolute TARGET.
146	
147	# References
148

# Bugs

Known defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for.
Each entry describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of
the failure. Entries are numbered (`1.` `2.` …) the same way
as `## Todo` in `todo.md`; run
`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/bugs.md` to renumber after
insert / delete / reorder.

## Bugs

1. **`vc-x1 push`: the session-repo push silently never happens
   when the bot runs push sandboxed.** Observed in
   [tprobe](https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe) 2026-07-14:
   both local repos consistent and the GitHub code repo current,
   but [tprobe.claude](https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe.claude)
   stuck 8 commits behind (`jj bookmark list --all-remotes -R
   .claude`: `main@origin (behind by 8 commits)`).
   - **Root cause (diagnosed 2026-07-14).** Push doesn't push
     `.claude` itself; its `finalize-claude` stage spawns a
     detached child that sleeps 10s first:
     - the child — `vc-x1 finalize --squash --push main
       --delay 10 --detach` — is `setsid`'d to outlive push
     - a sandboxed Bash command kills every process in its pid
       namespace at command exit; `setsid` escapes the process
       group, not the namespace
     - the child dies mid-sleep, before its first `jj` command
     - reproduced in this project's sandbox with a bare `setsid`
       child; the bot thinks the same kill explains tprobe,
       whose sandbox is always-on (`autoAllowBashIfSandboxed:
       true`, `allowUnsandboxedCommands: false`) with
       `vc-x1 push *` permission-allowed
   - **Evidence.**
     - tprobe app repo: 8 `push bookmark main` ops — the
       in-process `push-app` stage worked; network was fine
     - tprobe `.claude`: zero squash/push ops since clone — the
       child never ran even one command
     - tprobe `.vc-x1/` state dir empty — every push "completed
       all stages"
   - **Why it's silent.**
     - SIGKILL — the child never writes a failure marker
     - marker writes to `~/.cache/vc-x1` are outside the sandbox
       write allowlist anyway; `write_failure_marker` swallows
       errors by design
     - `verify_completion_sanity` checks local bookmarks only
     - related: `CliLogger::new` panics (`.expect`) when the
       `--log` path can't be opened — a sandbox denying
       `/tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log` kills the child at startup
   - **Side effect:** the trailing-data squash never runs; the
     tail rides into the next cycle's session commit (misplaced,
     not lost).
   - **Fix direction:** see the Todo "push: inline the
     session-repo push; finalize becomes the user's empty-@
     tidy-up".
   - **Recovery** for an affected project: `jj git push
     --bookmark main -R <proj>/.claude` from an unsandboxed
     shell (or any in-process command that finishes before its
     sandbox exits).

2. **`finalize` squash silently drops the source journal's
   ochids.** `finalize_exec` (`src/finalize.rs`) always runs
   `jj squash --ignore-immutable --use-destination-message
   --from <source> --into <target>`, so when the source
   commit's message carries `ochid:` trailers the squash
   discards them with the rest of the message — the
   destination's message wins unconditionally.
   - **When it bites:** the squash source (`@`) is a
     *described* journal instead of a bare trailing-data
     snapshot. Observed sequence (fc op log, 2026-06-08):
     the bot wrote the journal message via
     `jj describe wyrlsuusnyzz` while that change was still
     the uncommitted `@`; finalize then squashed
     `--from @ --into @-` where `@-` was the previous,
     already-pushed journal — destination message won,
     journal message + 6 ochids discarded. Finalize's
     assumption (`@` is disposable, `@-` holds the real
     message) inverts whenever the journal is described on
     `@` rather than committed first.
   - **Cost:** every code-side commit pointing at the
     squashed journal is left with a dangling
     `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` — the code↔session cross-link
     breaks. Recovery requires op-log surgery on the machine
     that still has the original object (this happened in the
     fc project: journal `wyrlsuusnyzz`, 6 dangling app
     ochids, recovered 2026-06-10 from a backup workspace's
     op log plus a `.claude main` force-push).
   - **Fix direction:** before squashing, detect `ochid:`
     trailers in the source message that the destination
     lacks; refuse (or merge the trailers into the
     destination message) instead of dropping them.

3. **`finalize::surface_previous_failures` is racy and
   bounded by "next vc-x1 run".** The current model has
   four gaps:
   - **Stale forever.** Markers sit on disk until the
     next `vc-x1 <anything>` runs. If the user abandons
     the workspace (e.g., CI / scheduled use), failures
     are never surfaced.
   - **Concurrent surface_previous_failures.** Two
     `vc-x1` runs racing: both `read_dir`, both print
     the marker, one deletes (the other's `remove_file`
     silently fails). The user sees the same failure
     printed twice.
   - **Mid-write torn read.** A `finalize --exec` child
     writing a marker while a sibling `vc-x1` is
     surfacing could read partial content. Atomic-rename
     on write would close this.
   - **No notify-at-failure path.** A detached
     `finalize --exec` failure only becomes visible when
     the user next runs *any* `vc-x1` command — fine for
     interactive use, invisible for CI / scheduled use
     where there may be no next run.

   The exec-child gate in `main.rs` (since 0.52.0-3)
   patches one related case — the detached child eating
   its own prior markers before the user can see them —
   but doesn't address any of the above. Holistic fix
   needs locking + atomic writes + maybe a
   notify-at-failure path for hands-off use.

4. **`init --repo local` bare remotes keep HEAD at
   `refs/heads/master`.** The only branch pushed is `main`,
   so a later `jj git clone` of that bare repo has no default
   branch to auto-track and `vc-x1 clone` fails its
   `verify_tracking` check ("bookmark 'main' has non-tracking
   remote 'main@origin'"). Found building `tests/cli_sync.rs`
   (worked around there with `git symbolic-ref HEAD
   refs/heads/main`).
   - **Fix direction:** init's local bare provisioning sets
     HEAD to `refs/heads/main` at creation.

5. **`clone` session-remote derivation mismatches init's
   local naming; relative TARGET breaks the session clone.**
   Two related defects, both found building
   `tests/cli_sync.rs`:
   - `derive_session_url` maps `<x>.git` →
     `<x>.claude.git`, but `init --repo local` names the
     session remote `remote-claude.git` — so a dual clone of
     a locally-init'd project's `remote-code.git` looks for
     `remote-code.claude.git` and fails.
   - A relative local-path TARGET fails on the session side
     regardless: `clone_dual` runs the session `jj git
     clone` with the just-cloned code repo as cwd, so the
     relative source no longer resolves. Workaround: pass an
     absolute TARGET.

# References

# Bugs

Known defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for.
Each entry describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of
the failure. Entries are numbered (`1.` `2.` …) the same way
as `## Todo` in `todo.md`; run
`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/bugs.md` to renumber after
insert / delete / reorder.

## Bugs

1. **`vc-x1 push`: the session-repo push silently never happens
   when the bot runs push sandboxed.** Observed in
   [tprobe](https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe) 2026-07-14:
   both local repos consistent and the GitHub code repo current,
   but [tprobe.claude](https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe.claude)
   stuck 8 commits behind (`jj bookmark list --all-remotes -R
   .claude`: `main@origin (behind by 8 commits)`).
   - **Root cause (diagnosed 2026-07-14).** Push doesn't push
     `.claude` itself; its `finalize-claude` stage spawns a
     detached child that sleeps 10s first:
     - the child — `vc-x1 finalize --squash --push main
       --delay 10 --detach` — is `setsid`'d to outlive push
     - a sandboxed Bash command kills every process in its pid
       namespace at command exit; `setsid` escapes the process
       group, not the namespace
     - the child dies mid-sleep, before its first `jj` command
     - reproduced in this project's sandbox with a bare `setsid`
       child; the bot thinks the same kill explains tprobe,
       whose sandbox is always-on (`autoAllowBashIfSandboxed:
       true`, `allowUnsandboxedCommands: false`) with
       `vc-x1 push *` permission-allowed
   - **Evidence.**
     - tprobe app repo: 8 `push bookmark main` ops — the
       in-process `push-app` stage worked; network was fine
     - tprobe `.claude`: zero squash/push ops since clone — the
       child never ran even one command
     - tprobe `.vc-x1/` state dir empty — every push "completed
       all stages"
   - **Why it's silent.**
     - SIGKILL — the child never writes a failure marker
     - marker writes to `~/.cache/vc-x1` are outside the sandbox
       write allowlist anyway; `write_failure_marker` swallows
       errors by design
     - `verify_completion_sanity` checks local bookmarks only
     - related: `CliLogger::new` panics (`.expect`) when the
       `--log` path can't be opened — a sandbox denying
       `/tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log` kills the child at startup
   - **Side effect:** the trailing-data squash never runs; the
     tail rides into the next cycle's session commit (misplaced,
     not lost).
   - **Fix direction:** see the Todo "push: inline the
     session-repo push; finalize becomes the user's empty-@
     tidy-up".
   - **Recovery** for an affected project: `jj git push
     --bookmark main -R <proj>/.claude` from an unsandboxed
     shell (or any in-process command that finishes before its
     sandbox exits).

2. **`finalize` squash silently drops the source journal's

   destination's message wins unconditionally.
   - **When it bites:** the squash source (`@`) is a
     *described* journal instead of a bare trailing-data
     snapshot. Observed sequence (fc op log, 2026-06-08):
     the bot wrote the journal message via
     `jj describe wyrlsuusnyzz` while that change was still
     the uncommitted `@`; finalize then squashed
     `--from @ --into @-` where `@-` was the previous,
     already-pushed journal — destination message won,
     journal message + 6 ochids discarded. Finalize's
     assumption (`@` is disposable, `@-` holds the real
     message) inverts whenever the journal is described on

     squashed journal is left with a dangling
     `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` — the code↔session cross-link
     breaks. Recovery requires op-log surgery on the machine
     that still has the original object (this happened in the
     fc project: journal `wyrlsuusnyzz`, 6 dangling app
     ochids, recovered 2026-06-10 from a backup workspace's
     op log plus a `.claude main` force-push).
   - **Fix direction:** before squashing, detect `ochid:`
     trailers in the source message that the destination
     lacks; refuse (or merge the trailers into the

3. **`finalize::surface_previous_failures` is racy and

lastPrompt: Take a look at all the code and give me your opinion on its organization and the amount of code that is potnetially duplicated.

content.text:
Here's my assessment. Overall: the organization is unusually disciplined for a CLI this size (~16.7k lines); the duplication is real but concentrated in one pattern class, not smeared everywhere.

## Organization — the good

- **One module per subcommand + `SubcommandRunner`** gives uniform dispatch, and the clap-`Args` → clap-free `Params` layering is applied consistently across all 15 subcommands. This is the codebase's strongest structural idea.
- **`options_flags/`** (shared flag leaves like `SquashOption`, `RepoOption`, `ScopeOption`) reuses CLI surface cleanly.
- **`sync/` shows the right decomposition precedent**: `sync.rs` + `sync/state.rs` + `sync/tests.rs` + `sync/integration_tests.rs`.

## Organization — the strains

- **`push.rs` (1,579 lines) is the outlier.** It holds the `Stage` machine, TOML state persistence, eight stage bodies, two sanity verifiers, and the interactive gates. The `sync/state.rs` precedent suggests a `push/state.rs` (Stage + PushState + layout) split that would take ~400 lines out without changing anything's shape. `init.rs` (1,504) is second but decomposes less naturally.
- **`common.rs` (723 lines) is common-by-accretion**: subprocess helpers (`run`, `prompt`), jj-lib repo loading/revset/formatting, workspace-root/scope resolution, and bookmark-tracking checks all live there. Three files by concern (process, jj-query, workspace) would make each findable.
- **Two jj access styles coexist implicitly**: read-only display commands (`chid`, `desc`, `list`, `show`, `validate-desc`) use **jj-lib in-process**; mutating commands (`init`, `sync`, `push`, `squash-push`) **shell out** to `jj`. I think the split is actually defensible — rewrites via the CLI track jj's behavior more safely than library calls — but it's nowhere stated as a rule, so each new subcommand re-decides it.

## Duplication — where it actually is

1. **Ad-hoc `jj log -T <template>` queries — the big one.** The pattern `run("jj", &["log", "-r", rev, "--no-graph", "-T", <template>, "-R", repo], cwd)` appears at ~30 call sites, and each file has quietly grown its own private wrapper: `squash_push::jj_commit_id`/`jj_rev_exists`/`rev_is_empty_undescribed`, `push::jj_log_empty` plus four inline `change_id.short(12)` blocks, `init::jj_chid`, three template variants in `sync.rs`. Formatted, that's roughly 300–350 lines of boilerplate that a single typed facade (`jj_log(repo, rev, template)` plus `chid_of` / `cid_of` / `desc_of` / `is_empty` / `rev_exists`) would collapse to a few dozen — and give one home for error context.
2. **Test query helpers are defined three or four times.** `jj()`, `cid()`, `chid()`, `description()` exist near-identically in `push/integration_tests.rs`, `sync/integration_tests.rs`, and `tests/cli_sync.rs` (home-injecting variant). `test_helpers.rs` already exists as the shared harness — these belong beside `Fixture`.
3. **Bookmark checks are triplicated in style**: `squash_push` preflight's `bookmark list` existence check, `common::verify_tracking`'s parse via `find_non_tracking_remote`, and `main::bm_track_one` — which uses a *raw* `std::process::Command` (bypassing `run`) and its own `@origin:` prefix parse. Two different parsers for "is this bookmark tracking" is the kind of drift that eventually disagrees.
4. **Trailer parsing exists twice**: `squash_push::extract_ochids` (string-level) vs `common::extract_ochid` (jj-lib `Commit`-based), plus `repo_utils::cross_ref_ochids`. Small, but ochid semantics are load-bearing here — one string-level parser in `desc_helpers` that both call would be safer.

## My recommendation

No big-bang refactor — the doc-comment and layering discipline means the code reads well despite the size. Ranked by value:

1. **jj-query facade module** — kills the largest duplication class and touches every mutating subcommand's most fragile code (revset + template strings).
2. **Promote test query helpers into `test_helpers.rs`** — cheap, pure deletion.
3. **`push/state.rs` split** mirroring `sync/state.rs`.
4. Document the jj-lib-vs-subprocess rule in ARCHITECTURE.md; fold `bm_track_one` onto `verify_tracking`'s parser while there.

Want me to add these as `## Todo` entries (they're post-0.69.0 material — mid-cycle isn't the place to start them)?

message.content: Yes, I was looking at squash_push.rs and saw `fn jj_rev_exists` and thought this is the first time a function like that is needed, hence the question?

content.content:
120	  the inline session push; the bot sandbox now allows
121	  `~/.config/jj`, `/tmp/`, and `~/.cargo` writes, so tests
122	  and `cargo install` run sandboxed (fixed 2026-07-15)
123	
124	## Todo
125	
126	 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
127	 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
128	 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
129	 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
130	 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
131	 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
132	 number. Long-tail entries
133	 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
134	 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](/AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
135	 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
136	 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).
137	
138	1. **vc-x1 push: pause point between commit and publish
139	   stages.** The merge non-ff close-out is a three-step
140	   sequence:
141	   - commit the close-out pair locally (normal 1:1 commit
142	     stages, ochids injected both directions)
143	   - rebase the code-side close-out into the merge (chids
144	     survive rebase, so every ochid stays valid)
145	   - publish
146	
147	   Push has no supported stop after `commit-claude`, so today
148	   the recipe pre-commits both sides manually and resumes via
149	   `--from bookmark-set --yes` — skipping exactly the stages
150	   that inject `ochid:` trailers.
151	   - Add a stop after the commit stages (`--to commit-claude`
152	     or `--no-publish`; name open); the existing `--from
153	     bookmark-set` is already the resume half.
154	   - Retires the close-out workaround; the merge commit
155	     carries its code→bot ochid because it was injected
156	     normally, before the rebase.
157	   - Together with the Todo "push/sync: bookmark is
158	     code-repo-only; pin the bot repo to main", completes
159	     the trapezoidal-commit workflow (1:1 bot↔code
160	     throughout; the merge is a code-side-only shape
161	     operation).
162	
163	2. **Version-number protocol is fragile — versions are
164	   baked into titles/bodies/todo/done/chores before the
165	   change lands.** The cycle protocol embeds an `X.Y.Z-N`
166	   version in commit titles and bodies, `## Todo` /
167	   `## Done` entries, and chores headers — all written
168	   while the work is in progress, i.e. before it lands.
169	   But version numbers are subject to change: in a public,
170	   merge-based flow (e.g. Linux), the version a change
171	   ships under is only fixed when it merges into `main`,
172	   so the landing version can't be anticipated while the
173	   work is underway. Pervasive version-in-text is
174	   therefore fragile for any non-linear / multi-contributor
175	   workflow. Promoted from Ideas at 0.65.2-0; slated for
176	   the cycle after 0.65.2.
177	   - Open question: what identifies a cycle's commits if
178	     not a pre-assigned version?
179	     - Needs to be unique within some agreed upon domain.
180	       A contributors email address would do it, but also
181	       a UUID (short-version) for a contribution. I could
182	       imagine a UUID generated from the initial email/issue
183	       that and then "version number" schema appended to that.
184	   - Surfaces to update once the identifier is chosen:
185	     cycle-protocol.md (title shape, Numbering), AGENTS.md
186	     (commit-recording headers), and the `vc-x1` validators
187	     that parse `(X.Y.Z)` strings.
188	3. **sync follow-up: extract `move-bookmark` command.** The
189	   "put the bookmark / `@` where it belongs" step at the end
190	   of sync (reposition logic) is useful standalone — e.g. the
191	   t1B scenario where `main` is right but `@` isn't on it —
192	   and deserves an honestly-named command instead of a mode.
193	   - `vc-x1 move-bookmark` (name open): no fetch; move `@`
194	     (and optionally the bookmark) onto a target under the
195	     same safety rules as sync's reposition step.
196	   - Sync's final step becomes a call to the same logic.
197	   - Follow-up to the 0.67.0 single-mode sync cycle.
198	4. **sync follow-up: push preflight in-process; drop
199	   `--check`; revisit push auto-rollback.** Push's preflight
200	   shells out to `vc-x1 sync --check` — a verify-only pass
201	   that is both racy (remote can move before the user's
202	   later apply) and not actually read-only (jj's fetch
203	   auto-ffs tracked bookmarks). Follow-up to the 0.67.0
204	   single-mode sync cycle.
205	   - Preflight becomes a real in-process sync (stop-on-error
206	     halts the push before anything is committed); the
207	     `sync --check` shell-out and PATH dependency go away.
208	   - Remove the deprecated hidden `--check` alias once
209	     nothing invokes it.
210	   - Apply the stop-on-error + `vc-x1 revert` philosophy to
211	     push's commit-stage rollback (today it auto-runs
212	     `jj op restore`, hiding the evidence).
213	5. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all
214	   sequence-managed notes files generically.** `validate-todo`
215	   / `fix-todo` only operate on the single file passed, so a
216	   renumber slip in `bugs.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, or
217	   `todo.md`'s `## Ideas` section passes unnoticed — too weak
218	   for a pre-commit gate. Prereq for the pre-commit doc
219	   validators (Todo "pre-commit: single rule ...").
220	   - Rename the pair: `validate-todo` → `validate-numbering`,
221	     `fix-todo` → `fix-numbering` — they validate numbered-
222	     sequence integrity, not todos specifically.
223	   - Generic detection: for every `#…#` section, validate the
224	     column-0 `^\d+\.␠` entries form a contiguous 1..N run.
225	     Drops the Todo/Bugs special-casing; auto-covers
226	     `## Ideas` and any new numbered section. Keep the
227	     column-0 anchor so indented sub-lists aren't counted.
228	   - Default scope: a fixed list of sequence-managed notes
229	     files (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`) so the
230	     no-arg pre-commit run covers them all. Fixed rather than
231	     a `notes/**.md` walk because prose docs
232	     (`cycle-protocol.md`, design notes) carry ordinary
233	     numbered lists that aren't managed sequences — a walk
234	     would false-positive (markdown renders `1. 1. 1.` as
235	     1-2-3, a legitimate prose pattern).
236	   - Override args follow the `--init-from` convention:
237	     positional files/dirs (a dir → its `*.md`) plus an
238	     `@<file>` manifest, additive — for ad-hoc validation of
239	     a specific file.
